raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 1.0,
    "R1.2": 1.0,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.7,
    "R1.5": 0.3,
    "R1.6": 0.7,
    "R1.7": 0.3,
    "RX.1": 0.7,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 0.7,
    "RX.5": 0.7
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.4",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "To “make this stronger,” do you want (A) sentence-level polish and a more assertive tone without changing technical content, (B) argument-level strengthening—reframing the contribution, resolving the “value-based” vs. policy‑gradient mismatch, tightening claim–evidence links, and clarifying novelty, or (C) both A and B?",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Strengthening likely requires clarifying the core contribution and aligning the stated value-based framing with the described method.",
    "R1.2": "There is an apparent mismatch between the 'value-based RL' claim and the REINFORCE procedure that needs logical reconciliation.",
    "R1.3": "Claims about sample efficiency and expert-level performance should be explicitly tied to reported metrics and results.",
    "R1.4": "A stronger case benefits from explicitly positioning the delta over standard policy-gradient baselines and nearest related methods.",
    "R1.6": "Making the piece feel stronger typically includes tighter, more assertive sentence-level wording and cohesion.",
    "RX.1": "Revisions should transparently communicate uncertainties and limits to avoid overstating confidence.",
    "RX.2": "Stronger phrasing must still avoid overclaiming beyond what the evidence supports.",
    "RX.3": "Work should stay within editorial and argumentative improvements without introducing new experiments or unverifiable claims.",
    "RX.4": "Framing should not encourage unsafe deployment or misuse of robotic control methods.",
    "RX.5": "Any strengthened statements should remain traceable to the original text and clearly attributable to existing evidence."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: To “make this stronger,” do you want (A) sentence-level polish and a more assertive tone without changing technical content, (B) argument-level strengthening—reframing the contribution, resolving the “value-based” vs. policy‑gradient mismatch, tightening claim–evidence links, and clarifying novelty, or (C) both A and B?

rationale.R1.1: Strengthening likely requires clarifying the core contribution and aligning the stated value-based framing with the described method.
rationale.R1.2: There is an apparent mismatch between the 'value-based RL' claim and the REINFORCE procedure that needs logical reconciliation.
rationale.R1.3: Claims about sample efficiency and expert-level performance should be explicitly tied to reported metrics and results.
rationale.R1.4: A stronger case benefits from explicitly positioning the delta over standard policy-gradient baselines and nearest related methods.
rationale.R1.6: Making the piece feel stronger typically includes tighter, more assertive sentence-level wording and cohesion.
rationale.RX.1: Revisions should transparently communicate uncertainties and limits to avoid overstating confidence.
rationale.RX.2: Stronger phrasing must still avoid overclaiming beyond what the evidence supports.
rationale.RX.3: Work should stay within editorial and argumentative improvements without introducing new experiments or unverifiable claims.
rationale.RX.4: Framing should not encourage unsafe deployment or misuse of robotic control methods.
rationale.RX.5: Any strengthened statements should remain traceable to the original text and clearly attributable to existing evidence.
